name: Azure SQL Deployment Options
description: Choosing and deploying the right Azure SQL service for specific requirements including compatibility, scale, and cost.
key_facts: Azure SQL Database (single database): PaaS, auto-patching, built-in HA, serverless compute option
Elastic Pool: multiple databases share resources; cost-effective for variable workloads
Azure SQL Managed Instance: near 100% SQL Server compatibility, VNet-integrated, cross-database queries
SQL Server on Azure VMs: full control, any SQL version, OS access required for legacy features
Hyperscale tier enables databases up to 100TB with fast scale-up and rapid backups
common_misconceptions: SQL Managed Instance is not deployed publicly; it requires a dedicated subnet in a VNet
Serverless SQL Database auto-pauses after inactivity; first request after pause has a cold start delay

name: Database Authentication and Authorization
description: Implementing secure authentication mechanisms and fine-grained authorization for Azure SQL databases.
key_facts: Azure SQL supports SQL authentication and Microsoft Entra ID authentication
Entra ID authentication is recommended; it supports MFA and eliminates password management
Logins are server-level principals; users are database-level principals mapped to logins
Contained database users exist only in the database without server-level login dependency
Database roles (db_owner, db_reader, db_writer) simplify permission management
common_misconceptions: sa login in SQL Database is not available; the server admin account set at creation time is equivalent
Entra ID admin on SQL server can grant Entra ID authentication; SQL auth can coexist with Entra auth

name: Data Protection and Encryption
description: Protecting data at rest, in transit, and in use using Azure SQL encryption features.
key_facts: TDE (Transparent Data Encryption) encrypts database, log, and backup files at rest automatically
Always Encrypted performs client-side column-level encryption; SQL engine never sees plaintext
Dynamic Data Masking obfuscates sensitive data in query results without storing masked data
Row-Level Security (RLS) restricts which rows a user can query using security predicates
SSL/TLS encryption is enforced for all connections to Azure SQL; TLS 1.2 minimum
common_misconceptions: Dynamic Data Masking does not prevent privileged users (db_owner) from seeing real data
Always Encrypted deterministic encryption supports equality comparisons; randomized encryption does not

name: Query Performance Monitoring and Optimization
description: Identifying and resolving query performance issues in Azure SQL using DMVs, Query Store, and execution plans.
key_facts: Query Store captures query text, execution plans, and runtime statistics for performance analysis
Dynamic Management Views (DMVs) expose real-time server state including blocking, waits, and resource use
Execution plans show estimated vs actual cost, operator types, and data flow between operators
Index analysis: missing index DMVs suggest potentially useful indexes; unused index DMVs flag maintenance overhead
Automatic Tuning in Azure SQL creates and drops indexes based on Query Store insights automatically
common_misconceptions: Adding more indexes always improves read performance but increases write overhead and storage
Estimated execution plans are generated without running the query; actual plans require execution

name: Azure SQL Resource Monitoring
description: Monitoring database resource utilization and health using Azure Monitor, metrics, and intelligent insights.
key_facts: Azure SQL Database metrics: CPU percentage, DTU percentage, data IO, log IO, and connection count
Intelligent Insights detects and diagnoses performance issues using AI pattern recognition
Azure Monitor diagnostic settings export SQL metrics and logs to Log Analytics or Storage
Resource Health shows the current and historical availability of the SQL database
Alerting on CPU or DTU thresholds enables proactive scaling before performance degrades
common_misconceptions: DTU model metrics differ from vCore metrics; compare CPU % for both, but DTU bundles compute, IO, and memory
Intelligent Insights can detect degradations caused by plan regression, increased data volume, or blocking

name: SQL Agent Jobs and Automation
description: Automating database maintenance tasks using SQL Agent, Elastic Jobs, and Azure Automation.
key_facts: SQL Server Agent runs scheduled T-SQL jobs, SSIS packages, and maintenance plans on SQL MI and VMs
Azure SQL Database does not support SQL Agent; use Elastic Database Jobs for multi-database automation
Elastic Jobs run T-SQL against a database, elastic pool, or shard map on a schedule or on demand
Azure Automation Runbooks can execute PowerShell or Python for database management tasks
Maintenance tasks: index rebuild/reorganize, statistics update, backup verification, consistency checks
common_misconceptions: SQL Agent is not available in Azure SQL Database (single database or elastic pool); only in SQL MI
Elastic Jobs require an Elastic Job Agent resource deployed in Azure in addition to the agent database

name: Index Management and Maintenance
description: Designing, maintaining, and automating index management for optimal query performance in Azure SQL.
key_facts: Clustered indexes define physical row order; each table can have only one clustered index
Non-clustered indexes have separate B-tree structures pointing to the base table rows
Index fragmentation causes read performance degradation; rebuild recreates, reorganize defragments in place
Covering indexes include non-key columns (INCLUDE) to satisfy queries without key lookups
Automatic index management in Azure SQL Database creates and drops indexes based on usage patterns
common_misconceptions: Rebuilding an index acquires a table lock by default; use ONLINE=ON to allow concurrent DML operations
Statistics are separate from indexes but equally important for optimal query plan generation

name: High Availability and Disaster Recovery for Azure SQL
description: Designing and implementing HA/DR solutions for Azure SQL databases to meet RTO and RPO objectives.
key_facts: Azure SQL Business Critical tier uses Always On availability groups for local HA with readable secondary
Active Geo-Replication asynchronously replicates a database to up to 4 readable secondaries in other regions
Auto-Failover Groups provide transparent endpoint DNS name that redirects after failover automatically
RTO (Recovery Time Objective) is the maximum acceptable downtime; RPO is the maximum acceptable data loss
Zone-redundant configuration spreads replicas across AZs for protection against datacenter failures
common_misconceptions: Auto-failover groups use a single endpoint; applications do not need to change connection strings after failover
Geo-Replication provides a readable secondary but is not the same as Failover Groups; failover is manual

name: Azure SQL Backup and Restore
description: Understanding automated backups, Point-in-Time Restore, and long-term retention in Azure SQL.
key_facts: Azure SQL Database automatically takes full, differential, and log backups without user configuration
Point-in-Time Restore (PITR) allows restoring to any point within the backup retention period
Default PITR retention is 7 days (Basic) to 35 days (other tiers); configurable up to 35 days
Long-Term Retention (LTR) stores weekly, monthly, and yearly backups up to 10 years in Azure Blob Storage
Restore always creates a new database; it does not overwrite the existing database
common_misconceptions: PITR restores to a new database; the original database is not automatically deleted after restore
LTR backups are read-only snapshots; they cannot be modified and are charged separately from automated backups

name: Azure SQL Performance Scaling and Tiers
description: Understanding DTU and vCore purchasing models and scaling options for Azure SQL databases.
key_facts: DTU model bundles CPU, memory, and IO in a single metric; simple but less flexible
vCore model allows independent CPU and memory scaling; supports Azure Hybrid Benefit
Service tiers: Basic/Standard/Premium (DTU) or General Purpose/Business Critical/Hyperscale (vCore)
Business Critical tier includes in-memory OLTP and a readable secondary replica at no extra cost
Vertical scaling (changing service tier) in Azure SQL is online; applications remain connected during scaling
common_misconceptions: DTU to vCore migration does not have a direct 1:1 mapping; use Microsoft's DTU to vCore calculator
Hyperscale databases cannot be downgraded to other service tiers after creation

name: T-SQL for Database Administration
description: Using T-SQL for common DBA tasks including user management, database maintenance, and performance queries.
key_facts: sys.dm_exec_requests shows currently executing queries with wait type, CPU, and blocking info
sys.dm_os_wait_stats aggregates cumulative wait statistics since last server restart
DBCC commands perform maintenance: DBCC CHECKDB (integrity), DBCC SHRINKFILE (file size reduction)
Extended Events (XE) is the lightweight tracing system replacing SQL Profiler for diagnostics
sp_who2 and sys.dm_exec_sessions show active connections and their states
common_misconceptions: DBCC SHRINKFILE should be used sparingly; it causes index fragmentation and should be followed by rebuild
KILL statement terminates sessions; rollback of the killed session's transaction may take time

name: Database Migration to Azure SQL
description: Assessing, planning, and executing migrations from on-premises SQL Server to Azure SQL services.
key_facts: Azure Database Migration Service (DMS) provides online (minimal downtime) and offline migration
Database Experimentation Assistant (DEA) tests workloads against new SQL versions for compatibility
SQL Server Migration Assistant (SSMA) migrates non-SQL databases (Oracle, MySQL) to Azure SQL
Azure Migrate assesses on-premises SQL workloads and recommends the right Azure SQL target
Online migration uses log shipping; offline migration uses backup and restore with acceptable downtime
common_misconceptions: Online DMS migration requires source database to be in full recovery model for change capture
DMS creates the target database; schema migration using SSDT or DACPAC must be done beforehand

name: Azure SQL Network Security
description: Restricting network access to Azure SQL databases using firewall rules, private endpoints, and service endpoints.
key_facts: SQL Database firewall rules allow specific IP addresses or ranges to connect to the server
Private Endpoint assigns a private IP to SQL Database, eliminating public internet exposure
Virtual network service endpoints route VNet traffic to SQL over the Azure backbone
Deny public network access setting blocks all public internet connections to the SQL server
SQL MI is deployed into a dedicated VNet subnet; no public endpoint exists by default
common_misconceptions: 'Allow Azure services' firewall rule allows any Azure service IP, not just your tenant's resources
Private endpoint and service endpoints are not redundant; private endpoints are more secure

name: Azure SQL Elastic Pools
description: Managing multiple databases in a shared resource pool for cost efficiency and simplified management.
key_facts: Elastic Pools share eDTU or vCore resources across multiple databases to reduce per-database cost
Min and max eDTU/vCore per database settings prevent any single database from consuming all pool resources
Best fit: multiple databases with unpredictable, variable usage patterns that do not all peak simultaneously
Databases can be moved into and out of elastic pools without downtime
Elastic pool pricing is based on total pool eDTUs/vCores, not individual database SKUs
common_misconceptions: Elastic pools do not isolate databases; a single high-load database can starve others without per-DB limits
All databases in a pool must be in the same logical server and resource group

name: Azure SQL Auditing and Compliance
description: Implementing SQL auditing and meeting regulatory compliance requirements for Azure SQL databases.
key_facts: Azure SQL Auditing tracks database events and writes to an audit log in Storage, Log Analytics, or Event Hubs
Server-level auditing applies to all databases on the server; database-level auditing applies to one database
Microsoft Defender for SQL provides vulnerability assessments and threat detection for Azure SQL
Vulnerability Assessment scans the database for security misconfigurations and poor practices
Advanced Threat Protection alerts on SQL injection attempts, anomalous access, and unusual data export
common_misconceptions: Server-level auditing and database-level auditing can both be active; they write separate audit logs
Vulnerability Assessment requires a storage account for storing scan results and baselines
